subject: Mathematics
question: The value of $^{3n}C_n - ^{n}C_1 \cdot ^{3n-3}C_n + ^nC_2 \cdot ^{3n-6}C_n - ^nC_3 \cdot ^{3n-9}C_n + \ldots$ is equal to :-
solution:
Let required sum $= S =$ coeff. of $x^n$ in

$$(1+x)^{3n} - {}^nC_1(1+x)^{3n-3} + {}^nC_2(1+x)^{3n-6}$$

$$- {}^nC_3(1+x)^{3n-9} + \ldots\ldots$$

$$= \text{Coeff. of } x^n \text{ in } \left\{(1+x)^3 - 1\right\}^n$$

$$= \text{Coeff. of } x^n \text{ in } x^n(x^2+3x+3)^n$$

$$= 3^n$$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $R = (5\sqrt{5} + 11)^{2n+1}$ and $f = R - [R]$ where $[\ ]$ denotes the greatest integer function. Then the value of $Rf$ is
question: Let $R = (5\sqrt{5} + 11)^{2n+1}$ and $f = R - [R]$ where $[\ ]$ denotes the greatest integer function. Then the value of $Rf$ is
solution:
We are given $R = (5\sqrt{5} + 11)^{2n+1}$, and $f$ is just the part of $R$ left over after removing its whole number part. So $f$ is the fractional part of $R$.

Let's call the whole number part $I$.

$$R = I + f = (5\sqrt{5}+11)^{2n+1}, \text{ where } 0 < f < 1$$

Let us name this new quantity $f^1$:

$$f^1 = (5\sqrt{5}-11)^{2n+1}, \quad \text{so } 0 < f^1 < 1$$

Now subtract $f^1$ from $R$. When we expand both terms using the binomial theorem, all the terms with even powers of $5\sqrt{5}$ cancel out, and the surviving terms double up:

$$I+f-f^1=(5\sqrt{5}+11)^{2n+1}-(5\sqrt{5}-11)^{2n+1}$$

$$=2\left({}^{2n+1}C_{1}\cdot(5\sqrt{5})^{2n}\times 11+{}^{2n+1}C_{3}(5\sqrt{5})^{2n-2}\times 11^{3}+\ldots\right)$$

$$=\text{ an even integer } \rightarrow (1)$$

Now look closely. The left side is $I + f - f^1$. Since $I$ is already an integer and the total is an integer, the piece $f - f^1$ must also be an integer.

But both $f$ and $f^1$ lie between $0$ and $1$. So their difference $f - f^1$ lies between $-1$ and $1$. The only integer in that range is $0$.

$$\Rightarrow f - f^1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = f^1$$

Now we can find $Rf$ easily. Since $f = f^1$, we replace $f$ with $f^1$ and multiply:

$$\therefore Rf=Rf^1=(5\sqrt{5}+11)^{2n+1}\cdot(5\sqrt{5}-11)^{2n+1}$$

$$=(125-121)^{2n+1}$$

$$=4^{2n+1}$$

subject: Mathematics
q: The sum of coefficients of odd powers of $x$ in the expansion of $(1 + x + x^2 + x^3)^5$ is equal to
question: The sum of coefficients of odd powers of $x$ in the expansion of $(1 + x + x^2 + x^3)^5$ is equal to
solution:
Take

$$ P(x)=(1+x+x^2+x^3)^5 $$

We want the sum of coefficients of odd powers of $x$.

For any polynomial $P(x)$,

  • sum of coefficients of all terms is $P(1)$

  • sum of coefficients of even powers minus sum of coefficients of odd powers is $P(-1)$

So, if the required sum is $S_{\text{odd}}$, then

$$ S_{\text{odd}}=\frac{P(1)-P(-1)}{2} $$

Now find these values.

$$ P(1)=(1+1+1+1)^5=4^5=1024 $$

Next,

$$ P(-1)=(1-1+1-1)^5=0^5=0 $$

So,

$$ S_{\text{odd}}=\frac{1024-0}{2}=512 $$

Hence, the answer is

$$ \boxed{512} $$

Option B.

subject: Mathematics
question: $^{26}C_0 + ^{26}C_1 + ^{26}C_2 + \ldots + ^{26}C_{13}$ is equal to :
solution:
The binomial expression : $\boldsymbol{B}_{26}=\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_0\right)+\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_1\right)+\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_2\right)+\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_3\right)+\ldots . .+\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{13}\right)$ .........(i)

We know,

$$ \begin{aligned} & (1+x)^n={ }^n C_0 \cdot x^0+{ }^n C_1 \cdot x^2+\cdots \cdot \cdot+{ }^n C_n \cdot x^n \\ & \text{Put} ,x=1 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(1+1)^n={}^n{C_0}+{ }^n C_1+{ }^n C_2+\cdots+{}^n{C_n} \\ & \Rightarrow 2^n={}^n{C_0}+{}^n{C_1}+{}^n{C_2}+\cdots+{}^n{C_n} \end{aligned} $$

Substitute $\boldsymbol{n}=26: 2^{26}=\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_0\right)+\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_1\right)+\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_2\right)+\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_3\right)+\ldots \ldots+\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{26}\right)$ ........(ii)

From the standard formulae : $\left({ }^n \boldsymbol{C}_{\boldsymbol{r}}\right)=\left({ }^n \boldsymbol{C}_{\boldsymbol{n}-\boldsymbol{r}}\right)$ we can equate $\left\{\begin{array}{l}\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{14}\right)=\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{12}\right) \\ \left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{15}\right)=\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{11}\right) \\ \left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{16}\right)=\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{10}\right) \\ \left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{17}\right)=\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_9\right) \ldots . .(\boldsymbol{i} \boldsymbol{i} \boldsymbol{i}) \\ \vdots \\ \vdots \\ \vdots \\ \vdots \\ \left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{26}\right)=\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_0\right)\end{array}\right.$

From (i), $\boldsymbol{B}_{26}=\sum\limits_{r=0}^{13}\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_r\right) \ldots \ldots$ (iv)

From (iii), $\sum\limits_{r=14}^{26}\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_r\right)=\sum\limits_{r=0}^{12}\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{12-r}\right)=\sum\limits_{r=0}^{12}\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_r\right) \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{v})$

From (ii), (iv) and (v) :

$2^{26}=\sum\limits_{r=0}^{26}\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_r\right)=\sum\limits_{r=0}^{13}\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_r\right)+\sum\limits_{r=14}^{26}\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_r\right)$

$=\sum\limits_{r=0}^{13}\left({ }^{26} C_r\right)+\sum\limits_{r=0}^{12}\left({ }^{26} C_r\right)$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\sum_{r=0}^{13}\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_r\right)+\sum_{r=0}^{13}\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_r\right)-\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{13}\right) \\ & =2 \sum_{r=0}^{13}\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_r\right)-\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{13}\right) \\ & = 2 \boldsymbol{B}_{26}-\left({ }^{26} \boldsymbol{C}_{13}\right) \end{aligned} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\boldsymbol{B}_{26}$ = $2^{25}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left({ }^{26} C_{13}\right)$

subject: Mathematics
question: The value of the expression $\log_2 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{2} \sum\limits_{k=1}^{11} {^{12}C_k} \right)$ :
solution:
Given, The binomial expansion : $\boldsymbol{B}_{\boldsymbol{k}}=\log _2\left\{1+\frac{1}{2} \sum\limits_{\boldsymbol{k}=1}^{11}\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_{\boldsymbol{k}}\right)\right\}$

We know, Binomial Expansion : $(1+x)^n=1+\left({ }^n C_1\right) x+\left({ }^n C_2\right) x^2+\left({ }^n C_3\right) x^3+\ldots . .+x^n=\sum\limits_{k=0}^n\left({ }^n C_k\right) x^k$

Substitute $\left\{\begin{array}{l}x=1 \\ n=12\end{array}\right.$ above formula :

$(1+1)^{12} = \sum\limits_{k=0}^{12}\left({ }^{12} C_k\right)=\left({ }^{12} C_0\right)+\sum\limits_{k=1}^{11}\left({ }^{12} C_k\right)+\left({ }^{12} C_{12}\right)$

$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow 2^{12} =1+\sum_{k=1}^{11}\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_k\right)+1 \\ \Rightarrow 2^{12}-2 =\sum_{k=1}^{11}\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_k\right) \\ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left(2^{12}-2\right) =\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left\{\sum_{k=1}^{11}\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_k\right)\right\}\end{aligned}$

$\Rightarrow 1+\frac{1}{2}\left(2^{12}-2\right)=1+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left\{\sum\limits_{k=1}^{11}\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_k\right)\right\}$

$\Rightarrow 2^{11}=1+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left\{\sum\limits_{k=1}^{11}\left({ }^{12} \boldsymbol{C}_k\right)\right\}$

$\Rightarrow \log _2\left(2^{11}\right)=\log _2\left[1+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left\{\sum\limits_{k=1}^{11}\left({ }^{12} C_k\right)\right\}\right]$

$\Rightarrow 11\left(\log _2 2\right)=\log _2\left[1+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left\{\sum\limits_{k=1}^{11}\left({ }^{12} C_k\right)\right\}\right]$

$\Rightarrow 11=\log _2\left[1+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left\{\sum\limits_{k=1}^{11}\left({ }^{12} C_k\right)\right\}\right]$

subject: Mathematics
question: The expression $({}^{10}C_0)^2 - ({}^{10}C_1)^2 + ({}^{10}C_2)^2 - \ldots + ({}^{10}C_8)^2 - ({}^{10}C_9)^2 + ({}^{10}C_{10})^2$ equals :
solution:
Given, The binomial expression :

$\boldsymbol{S}_{10}=\left({ }^{10} \boldsymbol{C}_0\right)^2-\left({ }^{10} \boldsymbol{C}_1\right)^2+\left({ }^{10} \boldsymbol{C}_2\right)^2-\left({ }^{10} \boldsymbol{C}_3\right)^2+\ldots . .+\left({ }^{10} \boldsymbol{C}_{10}\right)^2 \ldots .$.(i)

$\begin{aligned} & (1+x)^n=C_0+C_1 x+C_2 x^2+C_3 x^3+\ldots . .+C_n x^n \ldots . . \quad(i i) \\\\ & (1-x)^n=C_0-C_1 x+C_2 x^2-C_3 x^3+\ldots . .+(-1)^n C_n x^n \ldots . .(i i i) \\\\ & \text { Substiute } n=10 \text { in ( } i i) \text { and ( } i i i)\end{aligned}$

$(1+x)^{10}=C_0+C_1 x+C_2 x^2+C_3 x^3+\ldots . .+C_{10} x^{10} \ldots \ldots(i v)$

$(1-x)^{10}=C_0-C_1 x+C_2 x^2-C_3 x^3+\ldots . .+C_{10} x^{10}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =C_{10}-C_9 x+C_8 x^2-C_7 x^3+\ldots \ldots+C_0 x^{10} \left[\because\left({ }^{10} C_r\right)=\left({ }^{10} C_{10-r}\right)\right]\\\\ & =C_0 x^{10}-C_1 x^9+C_2 x^8-C_3 x^7+\ldots . .+C_{10} \ldots . .(v) \end{aligned} $$

$(i v) \times(v) \Rightarrow\left(1-x^2\right)^{10}=\left(C_0+C_1 x+C_2 x^2+C_3 x^3+\ldots . .+C_{10} x^{10}\right)\left(C_0 x^{10}-C_1 x^9+C_2 x^8-C_3 x^7+\ldots . .+C_{10}\right)$

$\begin{aligned}= & \left(C_{10} C_0\right) x^{20}-\left(C_9 C_0+C_{10} C_1\right) x^{19}+\left(C_8 C_0-C_9 C_1+C_{10} C_2\right) x^{18}+\ldots . \\ & + \\ & \left\{\left(C_0\right)^2-\left(C_1\right)^2+\left(C_2\right)^2-\left(C_3\right)^2+\ldots . .+\left(C_{10}\right)^2\right\} x^{10} \\ & - \\ & \left\{C_0 C_9-C_1 C_2+C_2 C_3-C_3 C_4+\ldots . .-C_9 C_{10}\right\} x^9+\ldots . .+C_0 C_{10}\end{aligned}$

From standard binomial expansion : $\left(1-\boldsymbol{x}^2\right)^{10}=\sum\limits_{r=0}^{10}(-1)^r\left({ }^{10} \boldsymbol{C}_r\right)\left(\boldsymbol{x}^2\right)^r$

= $\begin{aligned} & \sum_{r=0}^4(-1)^r\left({ }^{10} C_r\right)\left(x^2\right)^r + (-1)^5\left({ }^{10} C_5\right) x^{10}+\sum_{r=6}^{10}(-1)^r\left({ }^{10} C_r\right)\left(x^2\right)^r\end{aligned}$

Comparison coeffecients of powers of $x$, the following inference is made :

$$ \left\{\left(\boldsymbol{C}_0\right)^2-\left(\boldsymbol{C}_1\right)^2+\left(\boldsymbol{C}_2\right)^2-\left(\boldsymbol{C}_3\right)^2+\ldots .+\left(\boldsymbol{C}_{10}\right)^2\right\}=(-1)^5\left({ }^{10} \boldsymbol{C}_5\right)=-\left({ }^{10} \boldsymbol{C}_5\right) $$

subject: Mathematics
q: The coefficient of $x^n$ in $ \left( 1 - \frac{x}{1!} + \frac{x^2}{2!} - \frac{x^3}{3!} + \ldots + \frac{(-1)^n x^n}{n!} \right)^2 $ is equal to
question: The coefficient of $x^n$ in $ \left( 1 - \frac{x}{1!} + \frac{x^2}{2!} - \frac{x^3}{3!} + \ldots + \frac{(-1)^n x^n}{n!} \right)^2 $ is equal to
solution:
Coefficient of $\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}$ in

$$\left(1-\frac{x}{1!}+\frac{x^2}{2!}-\frac{x^3}{3!}+\ldots+\frac{(-1)^{n-1} x^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}+\frac{(-1)^n x^n}{n!}\right)\left(\frac{(-1)^n x^n}{n!}+\frac{(-1)^{n-1} x^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}+\ldots+-\frac{x}{1!}+1\right) $$

To get the coefficient of $x^n$, we take all pairs of terms whose powers add up to $n$.

$$ =\frac{(-1)^n}{n!}+\frac{(-1)^n}{1!(n-1)!}+\frac{(-1)^n}{2!(n-2)!}+\ldots++\frac{(-1)^n}{n!} $$

Each term has the common factor $(-1)^n$, because in every product the powers of $x$ add to $n$, so the signs also give $(-1)^n$.

$$ =\frac{(-1)^n}{n!}\left({ }^n C_0+{ }^n C_1+{ }^n C_2+\ldots . .+{ }^n C_n\right)=\frac{(-2)^n}{n!}$$

Here we used $$\frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!}={ }^n C_r,$$ so $$\frac{1}{r!(n-r)!}=\frac{{ }^n C_r}{n!}.$$ Also, $$ { }^n C_0+{ }^n C_1+{ }^n C_2+\ldots+{ }^n C_n=2^n.$$ So the coefficient becomes $$\frac{(-1)^n}{n!}\cdot 2^n=\frac{(-2)^n}{n!}.$$

subject: Mathematics
q: The coefficient of $x^r$ in the expansion of $(x+a)^{n-1} + (x+a)^{n-2} (x+b) + (x+a)^{n-3} (x+b)^2 + \ldots + (x+b)^{n-1}$, where $a-b=1$ is equal to
question: The coefficient of $x^r$ in the expansion of $(x+a)^{n-1} + (x+a)^{n-2} (x+b) + (x+a)^{n-3} (x+b)^2 + \ldots + (x+b)^{n-1}$, where $a-b=1$ is equal to
solution:
Let's look at the given expression. It's a sum of terms:

$(x+a)^{n-1} + (x+a)^{n-2} (x+b) + (x+a)^{n-3} (x+b)^2 + \ldots + (x+b)^{n-1}$

You can see this is a Geometric Progression (GP).

The first term of this GP is $A = (x+a)^{n-1}$.

The common ratio is $R = \frac{x+b}{x+a}$.

There are $n$ terms in this GP.

The sum of a GP with $n$ terms is given by the formula $S_n = A \frac{R^n - 1}{R - 1}$.

Let's substitute our values of $A$ and $R$ into the formula:

$$S_n = (x+a)^{n-1} \frac{\left(\frac{x+b}{x+a}\right)^n - 1}{\frac{x+b}{x+a} - 1}$$

$\frac{x+b}{x+a} - 1 = \frac{x+b - (x+a)}{x+a} = \frac{x+b-x-a}{x+a} = \frac{b-a}{x+a}$.

We are given that $a-b=1$, which means $b-a = -1$.

So the denominator becomes $\frac{-1}{x+a}$.

$$S_n = (x+a)^{n-1} \frac{\left(\frac{(x+b)^n}{(x+a)^n} - 1\right)}{\frac{-1}{x+a}}$$

So, $$S_n = (x+a)^{n-1} \frac{\frac{(x+b)^n - (x+a)^n}{(x+a)^n}}{\frac{-1}{x+a}}$$

$$S_n = (x+a)^{n-1} \times \frac{(x+b)^n - (x+a)^n}{(x+a)^n} \times \frac{x+a}{-1}$$

So, $$S_n = \frac{(x+a)^n}{(x+a)^n} \times \frac{(x+b)^n - (x+a)^n}{-1}$$

$$S_n = 1 \times [((x+a)^n - (x+b)^n)]$$

$$(x+a)^n = \binom{n}{0}x^n a^0 + \binom{n}{1}x^{n-1} a^1 + \ldots + \binom{n}{r}x^r a^{n-r} + \ldots + \binom{n}{n}x^0 a^n$$

The coefficient of $x^r$ in $(x+a)^n$ is $\binom{n}{n-r} a^{n-r}$. (Remember $\binom{n}{r} = \binom{n}{n-r}$).

Similarly, for $(x+b)^n$, the coefficient of $x^r$ is $\binom{n}{n-r} b^{n-r}$.

So, the coefficient of $x^r$ in $[(x+a)^n - (x+b)^n]$ will be the difference of these two coefficients:

$$ = \binom{n}{n-r} a^{n-r} - \binom{n}{n-r} b^{n-r} $$

$$ = \binom{n}{n-r} (a^{n-r} - b^{n-r}) $$

subject: Mathematics
q:
The coefficient of $x^n$ in the polynomial

$\left(x+{ }^{2 n+1} C_0\right)\left(x+{ }^{2 n+1} C_1\right)\left(x+{ }^{2 n+1} C_2\right) \ldots\left(x+{ }^{2 n+1} C_n\right)$
is equal to :
question:
The coefficient of $x^n$ in the polynomial

$\left(x+{ }^{2 n+1} C_0\right)\left(x+{ }^{2 n+1} C_1\right)\left(x+{ }^{2 n+1} C_2\right) \ldots\left(x+{ }^{2 n+1} C_n\right)$
is equal to :
solution:
We have the polynomial $(x + {}^{2n+1}C_0)(x + {}^{2n+1}C_1)(x + {}^{2n+1}C_2) \ldots (x + {}^{2n+1}C_n)$.

This is a product of $(n+1)$ brackets, each containing $x$ plus some binomial coefficient. When we expand this product, we get a polynomial of degree $(n+1)$.

To find the coefficient of $x^n$, notice that $x^n$ appears when we pick $x$ from $n$ of the brackets and the constant (the binomial coefficient) from the remaining $1$ bracket. So the coefficient of $x^n$ is simply the sum of all the binomial coefficients present in the brackets:

$$\text{Coefficient of } x^n = {}^{2n+1}C_0 + {}^{2n+1}C_1 + {}^{2n+1}C_2 + \ldots + {}^{2n+1}C_n$$

Recall the identity for the sum of all binomial coefficients:

$${}^{2n+1}C_0 + {}^{2n+1}C_1 + {}^{2n+1}C_2 + \ldots + {}^{2n+1}C_{2n+1} = 2^{2n+1}$$

Also, binomial coefficients are symmetric, meaning ${}^{2n+1}C_r = {}^{2n+1}C_{2n+1-r}$. Since $(2n+1)$ is an odd number, the coefficients from $r = 0$ to $r = n$ are exactly the mirror image of the coefficients from $r = n+1$ to $r = 2n+1$. So both halves are equal.

This means:

$${}^{2n+1}C_0 + {}^{2n+1}C_1 + \ldots + {}^{2n+1}C_n = \frac{1}{2} \times 2^{2n+1}$$

$$= 2^{2n}$$

So the coefficient of $x^n$ in the given polynomial is $2^{2n}$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $x=(5+2 \sqrt{6})^n, n \in N$, then find the value of $x-x^2+x[x]$, where $[.]$ denotes greatest integer function.
question: Let $x=(5+2 \sqrt{6})^n, n \in N$, then find the value of $x-x^2+x[x]$, where $[.]$ denotes greatest integer function.
solution:
We are given $x = (5 + 2\sqrt{6})^n$. The number $5 + 2\sqrt{6}$ is a little more than 9, so when we raise it to the power $n$, we get a number with a whole part and a decimal part. Our job is to find the value of $x - x^2 + x[x]$.

The trick here is to look at a partner expression. Notice that $5 - 2\sqrt{6}$ is the conjugate of $5 + 2\sqrt{6}$. Its value is a small positive number, less than 1. Let us call this partner $N = (5 - 2\sqrt{6})^n$. Since this is a positive number smaller than 1, we have $0 < N < 1$.

Now here is the useful part. When we add $x$ and $N$ together, all the terms with $\sqrt{6}$ cancel out, and we are left with a whole number. So:

$$(5 + 2\sqrt{6})^n + (5 - 2\sqrt{6})^n = 2k,\ k \in I$$

This means $x + N$ is an integer.

Let us write $x$ as its integer part plus its fractional part. The greatest integer function $[x]$ gives the whole part, and $\{x\}$ is the leftover decimal part. So $x = [x] + \{x\}$, where $0 < \{x\} < 1$. Putting this into the equation:

$$[x] + \{x\} + N = 2k$$

Since $[x]$ and $2k$ are both integers, the remaining piece $\{x\} + N$ must also be an integer. But both $\{x\}$ and $N$ lie between 0 and 1, so their sum lies between 0 and 2. The only integer it can be is 1. Therefore:

$$\{x\} + N = 1$$

This tells us that $N = 1 - \{x\}$, which we will use in a moment.

Now let us simplify the expression we actually want. Take $x - x^2 + x[x]$ and factor out $x$:

$$x - x^2 + x[x] = x - x(x - [x])$$

The part $x - [x]$ is just the fractional part $\{x\}$. So this becomes:

$$x(1 - \{x\})$$

Remember we found that $1 - \{x\} = N$. So this is simply $xN$:

$$xN = (5 + 2\sqrt{6})^n (5 - 2\sqrt{6})^n$$

Multiplying the bases first, $(5 + 2\sqrt{6})(5 - 2\sqrt{6}) = 25 - 24 = 1$. So the whole product is $1^n = 1$.

$$x - x^2 + x[x] = 1$$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\left({ }^{100} C_1\right)^2+2\left({ }^{100} C_2\right)^2+3\left({ }^{100} C_3\right)^2+\ldots . .+100\left({ }^{100} C_{100}\right)^2=\frac{N(200!)}{(100!)^2}$ then the sum of the positive integral divisors of $N^2$ is $\_\_\_\_$
question: If $\left({ }^{100} C_1\right)^2+2\left({ }^{100} C_2\right)^2+3\left({ }^{100} C_3\right)^2+\ldots . .+100\left({ }^{100} C_{100}\right)^2=\frac{N(200!)}{(100!)^2}$ then the sum of the positive integral divisors of $N^2$ is $\_\_\_\_$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $S_n=\sum\limits_{r=1}^n \sum\limits_{t=0}^{r-1}\left(\frac{1}{6^n}{ }^n C_r{ }^r C_t 4^t\right)$, then value of $l$ where $l=\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(1-S_n\right)$ is :
question: If $S_n=\sum\limits_{r=1}^n \sum\limits_{t=0}^{r-1}\left(\frac{1}{6^n}{ }^n C_r{ }^r C_t 4^t\right)$, then value of $l$ where $l=\sum\limits_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(1-S_n\right)$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & S_n=\sum_{r=1}^n\left(\frac{1}{6^n}{ }^n C_r\left(\sum_{t=0}^{r-1}{ }^r C_t 4^t\right)\right) \\ & \quad=\sum_{r=1}^n \frac{1}{6^n}{ }^n C_r\left[(1+4)^r-4^r\right] \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{6^n} \sum_{r=1}^n\left({ }^n C_r 5^r-{ }^n C_r 4^r\right) \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{6^n}\left[(1+5)^n-1-\left((1+4)^n-1\right)\right]=\frac{6^n-5^n}{5^n}=1-\left(\frac{5}{6}\right)^n \\ & \sum_{r=1}^{\infty} 1-S_n=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(\frac{5}{6}\right)^n=\frac{\frac{5}{6}}{1-\frac{5}{6}}=5\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\left(1+2 x+3 x^2\right)^{10}=a_0+a_1 x+a_2 x^2+\ldots \ldots+a_{20} x^{20}$, then $a_1$ equals :
question: If $\left(1+2 x+3 x^2\right)^{10}=a_0+a_1 x+a_2 x^2+\ldots \ldots+a_{20} x^{20}$, then $a_1$ equals :
solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \because\left(1+2 x+3 x^2\right)^{10}=a_0+a_1 x+a_2 x^2+\ldots . .+a_{20} x^{20} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad[1+x(2+3 x)]^{10}=1+{ }^{10} C_1 x(2+3 x)^1+{ }^{10} C_2 x^2(2+3 x)^2+\ldots . . \end{aligned} $$

To get the coefficient of $x$, we only need the term that contains exactly one $x$.

In the expansion, the term ${ }^{10} C_1 x(2+3x)$ gives the $x$ term.

Its $x$ part is ${ }^{10} C_1 \cdot x \cdot 2 = 10 \cdot 2x = 20x$.

All other terms contain $x^2$ or higher powers, so they do not contribute to the coefficient of $x$.

Comparing the coefficient of $x$ in both sides, then

$$ a_1=20 $$

subject: Mathematics
